channel.title: NYT > Technology
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Why the SpaceX IPO Will Affect Your 401(k), Like It or Not
item.description: Elon Musk’s rocket company, on the cusp of the largest initial public offering ever, will soon end up in index funds after rule changes by Nasdaq and other index providers.
item.dc:creator: Mike Isaac and Maureen Farrell

item.title: Gwynne Shotwell, Elon Musk’s No. 2 at SpaceX, Is the Company’s Steady Hand
item.description: Gwynne Shotwell, the president and chief operating officer, is the adult-in-the-room foil to Mr. Musk as SpaceX prepares for a blockbuster initial public offering.
item.dc:creator: Ryan Mac
item.media:description: Gwynne Shotwell, SpaceX’s president and chief operating officer, in Miami Beach last year during one of her infrequent public appearances. “I love working for Elon,” she has said.

item.title: Who’s Excited for SpaceX’s I.P.O.? Space Nerds.
item.description: Few are as delighted by the pending market debut as the vibrant community of space enthusiasts who want to push discovery of the final frontier.
item.dc:creator: Sheera Frenkel

item.title: Hot I.P.O. Summer + What Is A.I. Doing to Math? + HatGPT
item.description: “We are on track to see what might be the three biggest I.P.O.s of all time.”
item.dc:creator: Kevin Roose, Casey Newton, Whitney Jones, Rachel Cohn, Vjeran Pavic, Chris Wood, Dan Powell, Elisheba Ittoop, Marion Lozano and Rowan Niemisto
item.media:credit: Photo Illustration by The New York Times; Photo: Getty Images

item.title: People Love to Hate This Fake Private Equity Guy
item.description: Johnny Hilbrant Partridge has found social media fame as PE Guy, a character who embodies obnoxious entitlement.
item.dc:creator: Guy Trebay

item.title: SpaceX Has $30 Billion Deal to Provide Google With A.I. Computing Power
item.description: Elon Musk’s rocket company said Google would pay it $920 million a month, as it prepared for its initial public offering.
item.dc:creator: Kate Conger
item.media:description: SpaceX’s Starbase offices in Texas. The agreement with Google could help to establish SpaceX as an infrastructure provider for companies that are fiercely competing in the A.I. race.

item.title: Reid Hoffman to Leave Microsoft’s Board of Directors
item.description: The LinkedIn co-founder was a key bridge to Microsoft’s relationship with OpenAI, but he also came with some baggage.
item.dc:creator: Karen Weise and Cade Metz
item.media:description: Reid Hofman, above, has faced scrutiny because of his connections to the disgraced financier Jeffrey Epstein.

item.title: From Cow-Milking Robots to Weed-Zapping Lasers, Farmers Are Embracing A.I.
item.description: We asked three farmers to tell us how new technology is revolutionizing the way they work.
item.dc:creator: Coralie Kraft and Spencer Lowell
item.media:credit: Spencer Lowell for The New York Times
item.media:description: The machine uses high-resolution cameras to identify weeds.

item.title: Wary of U.S., Carney Bets on AI Strategy for Canada
item.description: The country on Thursday released a national artificial intelligence strategy that focuses on building its sovereign capability and protecting consumers.
item.dc:creator: Vjosa Isai
item.media:credit: Chris Young/The Canadian Press, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Prime Minister Mark Carney of Canada during a demonstration at a Toronto hospital on Thursday. “Canadian A.I. adoption will be prudent, pragmatic and pro-worker,” he said.

item.title: What Are A.I. Agents Actually Doing?
item.description: A San Francisco start-up called Arena found that people are most likely to use A.I. agents on the job, particularly if they are in the tech industry.
item.dc:creator: Cade Metz
item.media:credit: Brennan Smart for The New York Times

item.title: How LinkedIn Found Its Social Platform Era
item.description: Celebrities and paid influencers have transformed the career-focused platform, which for some users, has become a job unto itself.
item.dc:creator: Isabella Kwai
item.media:credit: Annie Flanagan for The New York Times
item.media:description: “This whole corporate, ‘move the needle,’ synergistic stuff — I think people are tired of it,” said Brooke Sweedar, a content creator on LinkedIn. “You have to do something else.”

item.title: The Do’s and Don’ts of Buying Used Tech Gadgets
item.description: With a shortage of some components driving up costs of consumer electronics, refurbished devices are an attractive alternative to buying new.
item.dc:creator: Brian X. Chen

item.title: The Small-Business Owners Managing Whole Armies of A.I. Employees
item.description: When you turn A.I. agents loose on your finances, email and customers, what could possibly go wrong?
item.dc:creator: Clive Thompson

item.title: Can These ChatGPT Ads Make You Love A.I.?
item.description: ChatGPT goes with heartwarming retro vibes to sell a product that has become a source of concern for most Americans.
item.dc:creator: Steven Kurutz
item.media:description: Billboards for ChatGPT, part of a new ad campaign, rise above a corner tavern in the Greenwich Village neighborhood of Manhattan.

item.title: ‘Teachers Are Going to Hate It’: How Social Media Apps Hooked Teens at School
item.description: Internal documents show how tech giants grabbed children’s attention throughout the day, a strategy that schools say has undermined education.
item.dc:creator: Jennifer Valentino-DeVries
item.media:credit: Yana Paskova for The New York Times
item.media:description: Social media companies’ push to reach students has often overshadowed concerns from parents and teachers.

item.title: SpaceX IPO to Be Largest Ever at $135 Share Price
item.description: The $135 share price means Elon Musk’s rocket maker is poised to exceed the 2019 initial public offering of Saudi Aramco in both valuation and money raised.
item.dc:creator: Ryan Mac
item.media:description: SpaceX’s Starship rocket lifted off last month during a test flight from Starbase in Texas.

item.title: European Union Outlines Plan to Reduce Dependence on American Tech
item.description: The 27-nation European Union outlined how it hopes to expand the region’s data centers, semiconductors and cloud computing capabilities.
item.dc:creator: Adam Satariano
item.media:description: European Union flags flutter outside the European Commission headquarters in Brussels.

item.title: Scientists Find Way to Supercharge Dangerous Computer ‘Worms’ With A.I.
item.description: Researchers at the University of Toronto showed how hackers could use artificial intelligence to create a program that could target any known flaw in the world’s computers.
item.dc:creator: Cade Metz
item.media:credit: Brendan George Ko for The New York Times
item.media:description: Nicolas Papernot, a professor at the University of Toronto, led a team that demonstrated a way of building a dangerous computer “worm” using artificial intelligence.

item.title: Trump Signs Executive Order Seeking Oversight of A.I. Models
item.description: The order, which signaled a shift from the hands-off approach the White House had previously taken toward A.I., followed debates over how to gain control of A.I. models without disrupting innovation.
item.dc:creator: Sheera Frenkel and Tripp Mickle
item.media:description: President Trump at the White House last week.

item.title: Meta Expands Safety Features for Teenagers
item.description: The changes, after Meta’s legal losses in two child safety cases, are aimed at limiting harmful content shown to teenagers on Instagram, Facebook and Messenger.
item.dc:creator: Eli Tan

item.title: Florida Sues OpenAI Over Chatbot Safety Concerns
item.description: The state became the first to sue the ChatGPT maker over claims that its technology posed a risk to children and that the company had failed to warn the public of dangers.
item.dc:creator: David McCabe
item.media:description: Florida Attorney General James Uthmeier, center, during an event last month in Miami.

item.title: Anthropic Files to Go Public, Setting Stage for Huge I.P.O.
item.description: The artificial intelligence company, which is racing OpenAI to the stock market, has seen explosive growth over the last year thanks largely to technology that can automatically write computer code.
item.dc:creator: Mike Isaac
item.media:credit: Jason Henry for The New York Times
item.media:description: Anthropic is one of the high-profile tech companies preparing to go public this year.

item.title: Is A.I. Replacing Tech Workers or Providing an Excuse for Job Cuts?
item.description: Tech industry layoffs are accelerating, and executives have been quick to say it’s because their companies are doing more with artificial intelligence, even when there may be more to it.
item.dc:creator: Kalley Huang

item.title: How Box Created 13 New Types of Jobs Because of A.I.
item.description: Box, a Silicon Valley software maker, expects to have more employees, not fewer, as it hires A.I. architects, A.I. solutions managers and other new A.I.-related positions.
item.dc:creator: Kalley Huang
item.media:credit: Peter Prato for The New York Times
item.media:description: “We ourselves are selling A.I. to our customers, so that’s actually causing us to need to hire more people,” said Box’s chief executive, Aaron Levie.

item.title: Chinese Military Sought Nvidia Chips for Years, Report Says
item.description: An analysis of six years of procurement records suggests that the People’s Liberation Army has openly tried to acquire restricted U.S. technology.
item.dc:creator: Ana Swanson and Tripp Mickle
item.media:description: China’s military has stepped up its efforts to acquire artificial intelligence chips despite U.S. restrictions, according to Wirescreen, a software platform.

item.title: China Aims A.I. at Predicting Who Could Pose a Political Risk
item.description: New research examines how a Chinese company struggled to develop its predictive surveillance technology while U.S. restrictions were in place.
item.dc:creator: Julian E. Barnes
item.media:credit: Lam Yik Fei for The New York Times
item.media:description: The skyline of Beijing. A Chinese firm is working on products that use A.I. to examine location data and internet use to predict who could do or say something critical of the government.
